stations.label: DECIMAL DAY BIRTH
stations.line: Fourteenth of February, they retired the pound and cut me loose — two dollars to the quid, a fresh name, and no bloody idea yet what I was worth.
stations.era_photo: A young woman standing outside a Sydney bank branch on a hot February morning, wearing a crisp 1966 shift dress and white gloves, holding a handful of the brand-new decimal coins; shot on Kodachrome colour film, slightly warm cast, deep saturated reds, the grain fine but unmistakably mid-sixties.

stations.label: THE FLOAT INTO OPEN WATER
stations.line: December, and Keating and Hawke threw me off the peg into open water — sink or swim, mate, and I learned fast that you survive by swimming, not by wishing for the shore.
stations.era_photo: A woman in her thirties on a Sydney harbourside dock at dusk, wearing a boxy 1983 power blazer with shoulder pads, wind pulling at her hair, arms crossed and jaw set; shot on early-eighties 35mm colour negative film, slightly muddy tones, visible grain, a faint blue evening light.

stations.label: THE BOTTOM AT FORTY-SEVEN
stations.line: Dot-com boom, and everyone wanted the shiny American story — I got dumped down under forty-eight US cents, the loneliest a currency can feel, but I dug in and waited.
stations.era_photo: A woman sitting alone on a red-dirt roadside somewhere in the outback, dusty boots, faded work shirt, a battered ute in the background under a vast empty sky; shot on early digital compact camera circa 2001, low resolution, slightly noisy in the shadows, flat contrast.

stations.label: THE GFC ABANDONMENT
stations.line: October — I'd been the golden girl of the carry trade, everyone borrowing me for yield, and then the whole thing turned risk-off and they dropped me thirty per cent in a matter of weeks, straight through the floor.
stations.era_photo: A woman standing at a rain-streaked office window overlooking a grey city skyline, wearing a dark tailored suit, phone pressed to her ear, expression carefully unbothered; shot on a 2008 DSLR, clean but cool-toned, sharp detail, the reflection of ticker-red numbers faint in the glass.

stations.label: ABOVE PARITY ON ROCKS
stations.line: Mining boom in full roar, China buying every tonne of iron ore we could ship, and for the first time I traded above the greenback — dollar-ten, and it was built on rocks in the ground, which is the only kind of value I trust.
stations.era_photo: A woman in high-vis and a hard hat standing at a Pilbara ore-loading port, red iron dust on everything, a Cape-size bulk carrier behind her, grinning into hard sunlight; shot on a 2011 DSLR, crisp and bright, strong saturated orange-red earth against deep blue sky.

stations.label: THE COVID FLASH CRASH
stations.line: March, and the world seized up overnight — I got sold down to fifty-five cents in the panic, that gut-drop you never forget, then rebuilt myself off the back of every commodity the planet still needed.
stations.era_photo: A woman alone in an empty airport terminal wearing a face mask pulled below her chin, casual travel clothes, harsh fluorescent overhead light, deserted seats stretching behind her; shot on a 2020 smartphone, clean high-resolution digital, slightly clinical white balance.

stations.label: STILL DIGGING TODAY
stations.line: Here now — still tied to what comes out of the ground, still swinging on China's mood and the price of ore, and still telling anyone who'll listen that what's real doesn't need a footnote.
stations.era_photo: A woman in her present day, weathered and steady, standing on a coastal cliff with the Indian Ocean behind her, plain shirt and sun-lined face, looking directly at the lens; shot on a 2025 mirrorless camera, ultra-sharp, natural colour, the light of late afternoon warm on her skin.
